@graph.name: Mercedes-Benz A180 CDI Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz A180 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned A180 CDI engine typically costs £3,000–£5,000 supply only, with the OM651 variant at the higher end. Fitting adds £800–£1,300 depending on the specialist. A used OM640.940 engine is available from approximately £617. A new OEM replacement engine can cost £2,500–£4,000+, with labour pushing the total above £5,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your A-Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz A180 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which engine era your A180 CDI has. The OM640 (W169, 2004–2012) is generally more straightforward and reliable. The OM651 (W176, 2012–2018) has well-documented timing chain tensioner issues — the simplex roller chain can stretch, and replacement is labour-intensive and costly. The OM607 (Renault K9K) suffers from turbocharger and injector issues. With proper maintenance, all can be reliable — but the OM651 requires proactive timing chain attention.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz A180 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issues are timing chain tensioner wear on the OM651 (rattling on cold start — can destroy the engine if ignored), injector failure on both OM651 and OM607 (rough idle, lumpy cold start), turbocharger failure on the OM607 (power loss, smoke, unusual noise), DPF clogging (short journeys accelerate wear), and EGR valve clogging.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an A180 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the vehicle value and engine era. Against a well-kept W176 worth £6,000–£12,000, a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 30–50% of the car's value — borderline but often worth it for a cherished example. Against a tired W169 worth £2,000–£4,000, the same recon may exceed our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold — get a scrap valuation first. OM640-engined cars are generally simpler and cheaper to maintain.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz A180 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: W169 (2004–2012): OM640.940 or OM640.941. W176 (2012–2018): OM607 (Renault-sourced 1.5 dCi) or OM651 (Mercedes 2.1-litre). Check the VIN plate or vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the engine block.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the OM651 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and tensioner must be replaced if worn. A preventative replacement costs £800–£2,000. A snapped chain almost always destroys the engine, requiring a £3,500–£6,500+ replacement. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes are the key to preventing timing chain issues.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: A-Class A180 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential maintenance — catch it before catastrophic failure
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Viable repair — catch it before it damages the engine
Standard maintenance on any modern diesel

buyingChecks.title: Buying This A180 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * OM640-engined W169 (2004–2012) with documented maintenance history
* OM651-engined W176 with timing chain tensioner replaced or inspected
* OM607-engined W176 with turbocharger history (and still under warranty)
* Full service history with regular oil changes (critical for chain longevity)
* Evidence of regular longer runs (DPF health)
avoidIf: * OM651 with cold-start rattle and no chain history — assume it's overdue
* OM607 with turbocharger issues or no history — turbo failure is common
* No service history — all A180 CDI engines are sensitive to oil quality
* Short-journey ownership history — DPF and EGR issues likely
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — may indicate timing, injector or turbo problems

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The A180 CDI is Mercedes-Benz's entry-level diesel hatchback — a 2.0-litre (W169) or 1.5/2.1-litre (W176) turbo-diesel that offers excellent fuel economy and a premium driving experience. The OM640 in the W169 is generally more straightforward, while the OM651 in the W176 has timing chain vulnerabilities that are expensive to address. The OM607 (Renault K9K) has turbocharger and injector issues. With typical A-Class values ranging from £3,000 (W169) to £12,000+ (W176), an engine replacement is a significant investment — a well-maintained W169 OM640 or a W176 with documented timing chain work is the safest choice.

cta.label: → Compare A180 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz A-Class A180 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling (timing chain tensioner warning — OM651) — see the map above
* Service History — Including which engine era (OM640, OM607 or OM651) and oil-change discipline
* Timing Chain — For OM651 (W176): check for any chain work or cold-start rattle
* Injector Health — Check for smooth idle; any lumpiness may indicate injector issues
* Turbo Health — Test acceleration; any lag, whistling, or hesitation? (OM607 particularly)
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on all diesel eras
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes read; unresolved issues may indicate timing, injector or turbo problems
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
